chunk_summary: This section outlines pediatric-specific nutrition strategies for chronic kidney and liver disease, emphasizing phenotype staging and prioritizing growth and lean mass preservation over restrictive diets that may harm development.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of disease phenotype staging in pediatric CKD and CLD nutrition planning.
Learn how to prioritize growth and lean mass preservation in pediatric patients with CKD and CLD.
Identify key differences in nutritional needs based on CKD stage, modality, and CLD complications.
Avoid common errors in pediatric nutrition by adapting adult dietary templates to pediatric needs.
key_facts: In CKD, staging is based on GFR category and markers of kidney damage, with modalities including conservative care, PD, HD, or post-transplant.
Indian pediatric CKD phenotypes often include polyuria and sodium wasting in CAKUT-like patterns, and edema/hypertension in glomerular diseases.
Growth and lean mass preservation are prioritized over fluid/electrolyte restrictions unless necessary to prevent imminent harm (e.g., hypertension, pulmonary edema).
SDI (Suggested Dietary Intake) is used for age-banded intake targets in pediatric kidney guidance.
Restrictive diets that improve short-term lab results can lead to irreversible height loss in children.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Start every plan by classifying the disease phenotype because targets differ more by stage and decompensation than by diagnosis label. (KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline)
document_key_topics: Phenotype staging in pediatric CKD and CLD
Growth-first nutrition goals in chronic disease
Avoiding restrictive diets in pediatric patients
Indian-specific CKD phenotypes and nutritional implications
